coredata.dc:title: An optical probe based on lanthanide light-emitting nanomaterials for detecting phosphate by ratiometric fluorescence method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Xue Zhen
Zhang, Tai Yi
Guo, Yu Rong
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: Inorganic phosphate (Pi) not only sustains the physiological functions within organisms but also acts as an indicator of environmental water pollution. Thus, it is extremely significant to seek out a rapid and straightforward method for detecting Pi. In this paper, the lanthanide metal luminescent nanomaterial europium-pyromellitic acid (Eu-PMA, exhibiting characteristic fluorescence emission at 435 nm for PMA and 617 nm for Eu3+) is synthesized via a simple solvothermal approach and utilized as a probe for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Pi. As the amount of Pi increases, Pi has a strong complexation ability with ligand PMA, resulting in the structural damage of Eu-PMA. PMA cannot sensitize Eu3+ luminescence, thus the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm of the system is markedly decreased. Meanwhile, through energy transfer interaction, the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm of ligand PMA is weakly increased. Based on the ratio of the changes in fluorescence intensities of the two, a ratiometric fluorescence method is established for the detection of Pi. The method is not only rapid and simple, but also improves the sensitivity of Pi detection (Linear range is 0.1–90 μM). Moreover, the content of Pi in actual water samples has been analyzed and determined successfully by this method, which further broads the significance and application prospect of the ratiometric fluorescence method in detecting Pi.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide metal luminescent nanomaterial
Ratiometric fluorescence method
Phosphate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Pi by ratiometric fluorescence method
Detection of Pi in actual samples
Feasibility and mechanism of Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence
Optimization of the experimental conditions for Pi detection
Detection of Pi using Eu-PMA by ratiometric fluorescence method
Detection of Pi in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of Eu-PMA. (B) FT-IR spectra of PMA and Eu-PMA. (C) XPS full spectrum of Eu-PMA. XPS spectra of each element in Eu-PMA of (D) C 1s, (E) O 1s, and (F) Eu 3d.
(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Eu-PMA in aqueous solution. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-PMA at different excitation wavelengths.
Feasibility experimental scheme of Eu-PMA to detect Pi.

Optimization of experimental conditions. (A) The difference in fluorescence intensity changes between blank solution and target Pi under different pH conditions. (B) The fluorescence intensity ratio changes of the blank and the target Pi-containing systems under different pH conditions. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Pi detected by the Eu-PMA system under different pH conditions (pH = 6.5–9.5). (D) Optimization of the response time of the Eu-PMA system to detect Pi.

Eu-PMA system for Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence method. (A) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-PMA system at different concentrations of Pi. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 435/I 617) and the concentration of Pi. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the Eu-PMA system under different potential interferers. (D) Selectivity study of the Eu-PMA system. (E) Fluorescence spectra of the Eu-PMA system in the presence of different potential interferers and target Pi. (F) Interference study of the Eu-PMA system.
Detection results of Pi in water samples from Jialing River (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An optical probe based on lanthanide light-emitting nanomaterials for detecting phosphate by ratiometric fluorescence method

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biomedical Diagnosis and Treatment Materials Engineering Research Center (Qilu Institute of Technology)
College of Biological and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biomedical Diagnosis and Treatment Materials Engineering Research Center (Qilu Institute of Technology), Shandong, College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Qilu Institute of Technology, Jinan 250200, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inorganic phosphate (Pi) not only sustains the physiological functions within organisms but also acts as an indicator of environmental water pollution. Thus, it is extremely significant to seek out a rapid and straightforward method for detecting Pi. In this paper, the lanthanide metal luminescent nanomaterial europium-pyromellitic acid (Eu-PMA, exhibiting characteristic fluorescence emission at 435 nm for PMA and 617 nm for Eu3+) is synthesized via a simple solvothermal approach and utilized as a probe for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Pi. As the amount of Pi increases, Pi has a strong complexation ability with ligand PMA, resulting in the structural damage of Eu-PMA. PMA cannot sensitize Eu3+ luminescence, thus the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm of the system is markedly decreased. Meanwhile, through energy transfer interaction, the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm of ligand PMA is weakly increased. Based on the ratio of the changes in fluorescence intensities of the two, a ratiometric fluorescence method is established for the detection of Pi. The method is not only rapid and simple, but also improves the sensitivity of Pi detection (Linear range is 0.1–90 μM). Moreover, the content of Pi in actual water samples has been analyzed and determined successfully by this method, which further broads the significance and application prospect of the ratiometric fluorescence method in detecting Pi.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic phosphate (Pi) not only maintains acid-base and electrolyte balance in the body [1–4], but also participates in bone mineralization, gene building, energy storage, and signal transmission. When there is an imbalance in it, a variety of diseases may occur, for example, osteomalacia, vitamin D deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, and renal insufficiency [5–9], and it is also an essential nutrient for aquatic organisms and plants [10]. Nevertheless, when phosphate is in excess (> 0.2 mg L−1), it can result in the eutrophication of natural water bodies, so it also acts as an indicator of water pollution [11,12]. Industrial or agricultural wastewaters as well as domestic sewage constitute the major sources for phosphate to enter aquatic ecosystems [13–15]. Hence, effective monitoring of Pi in the environment is of great practical significance.

To date, numerous methods have been devised for detecting phosphates, like colorimetry [16,17], chromatography [18], electrochemical analysis [19], plasma mass spectrometry [20]. These methods have the inherent disadvantages such as time-consuming sample pretreatment, expensive instruments, and lack of professional operators, which make it impossible to perform routine analysis. In contrast, fluorescence analysis has garnered significant attention because of its high performance, rapid response, low cost, high sensitivity, and ease of operation [9,21–23]. However, the current fluorescence detection methods of Pi are all based on the change of a signal, and the interference of instrument, environment and background on the performance change cannot be eliminated [24,25]. Fortunately, the ratiometric fluorescence method can effectively avoid these defects through self-calibration [23,26,27]. Hence, to meet the requirement for Pi detection, developing a low-cost, rapid, and reliable ratiometric fluorescence approach for sensitive detection of Pi in the environment is of particular significance.

At present, luminescent materials based on lanthanide ions are designed as ratiometric fluorescence probes due to their distinct optical characteristics, including high quantum yield, long fluorescence lifetime, large Stokes shift, and narrow emission spectrum [28–31]. Among them, the sensing system of lanthanide ion Eu3+ exhibits excellent luminescence performance and is utilized in the analysis and detection of biological and environmental samples. For instance, Guo et al. designed a novel luminescent MOF (Eu/Al-MOF) for sensing both histidine and water [27]. Kong et al. explored dual-ligand Eu-MOF (BTC-Eu-BDC-NH2) for detecting Hg2+ in water [32]. Yang et al. prepared CDS-doped Eu-MOFs for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in aqueous environment [33]. Xiao et al. prepared a logic device of dual Eu-MOFs and a ratiometric fluorescent paper microchip for visual H2O2 determination [34]. Based on this, we aim to construct lanthanide metal luminescent materials with Eu3+ as the center for detecting Pi by the ratiometric fluorescence method.

Inspired by the above, in this paper, Eu3+ was selected as the metal center, pyromellitic acid (PMA) was selected as the ligand, and the lanthanide luminescent material europium-pyromellitic acid (Eu-PMA) with excellent optical properties was synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Therefore, it was used as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting Pi. When Pi was added, the structure of Eu-PMA was destroyed due to the strong complexation ability of Pi with the ligand PMA, so that Eu3+ was no longer sensitized by PMA and no longer emitted at 617 nm. In the system, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 617 nm is significantly reduced, while the ligand PMA at 435 nm is weakly increased through energy transfer. Based on the ratio of the alteration in fluorescence intensity, a ratiometric fluorescence method was constructed for detecting Pi. This method is not only rapid and simple, but also can improve the sensitivity of Pi detection, and has been effectively utilized for the analysis and detection of Pi content in actual water samples.

An F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to determine the fluorescence spectrum. The photomultiplier voltage was set to 400 V. A JB400 filter was placed between the sample pool of the fluorometer and the detector to eliminate the effect of the secondary scattering peak. The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum was determined by using a UV-2450 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). A field emission scanning electron microscope ZEISS Gemini 300 (Zeiss, Germany) was used to take topography photographs of materials and record dark field Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. A Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray Photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Field, USA) was used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. A D2 Phaser X-ray diffractometer from Bruck in Germany was used for solid powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis. Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by employing a Bruker IFS 113v infrared spectrometer from Bruker in Germany. An FE28 digital pH meter (Mettler, Switzerland) was used to test and calibrate the pH of solutions.

Some inorganic salts, sodium phosphate (Na3PO4·12H2O), and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sichuan Chengdu Colon Chemical Reagent Factory (Sichuan, China). Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) and PMA were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). The actual samples were obtained from Jialing River in Chongqing, China. Reagents and drugs used in the experiment are analytically pure. The experimental water was 18.25 MΩ cm of ultra-pure water.

Eu-PMA was prepared by the one-pot solvothermal approach. To be more specific, 0.0670 g (0.15 mM) Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.0381 g (0.15 mM) PMA were added to a mixed solution containing 7 mL of DMF and 1 mL of H2O. After being completely dissolved, the mixture was transferred to the reactor and placed in an oven at 160℃ for 3 h. After the reaction was complete, it was cooled to room temperature, taken into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged for 5 min. After removing the mother liquor, the precipitate obtained was washed three times using DMF and then once using methanol to obtain the white precipitate. Then, it was placed in a vacuum drying oven at 60℃ and dried for 1 h to obtain the Eu-PMA solid powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pi by ratiometric fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 200 μL of 0.25 mg/mL Eu-PMA solution and 600 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution were added into a 1.5 mL centrifugation tube (pH = 7.5), then Pi of a certain concentration gradient was added to the centrifuge tube and the solution system was diluted to 1 mL with ultra-pure water. Subsequently, the system was incubated at room temperature for 20 min. Then, the system was transferred to the colorimetric dish with a pipette gun and placed in the detection chamber of a fluorometer. The fluorescence emission spectrum of the system was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 265 nm. Similarly, the selectivity and anti-interference performance of Pi system detected by this ratiometric fluorescence method were tested by selecting different potential disruptors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pi in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The actual water samples from the Jialing River in Beibei District (Chongqing, China) were selected. After the collection of Jialing River water samples, the supernatant was obtained through filtration, centrifugation and boiling treatment, and the Pi content in Jialing River water samples was detected and labeled for recovery by the ratiometric fluorescence method described in Section 2.4 as mentioned above.

The lanthanide metal luminescent material Eu-PMA was prepared by solvothermal method. Firstly, the structure and morphology of the material were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Eu-PMA showed an irregular lamellar structure (Fig. 1 A). According to Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterization, the position of CO stretching vibration peak of the ligand PMA moves from 1711.57 to 1591.12 cm−1 in the direction of low wave number, and the intensity decreases, when Eu-PMA is formed (Fig. 1B). This shows that the lanthanide luminescent material Eu-PMA has been synthesized by chelating the carboxyl group of PMA with Eu3+. To further illustrate, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization reveals the presence of elements Eu, C, and O in Eu-PMA (Fig. 1C), corresponding to Eu 3d, C 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 1D–F). All the results mentioned above have demonstrated that Eu-PMA was successfully prepared.

The lanthanide luminescent material Eu-PMA has good luminescence properties. By testing the fluorescence spectrum of Eu-PMA solid powder dissolved in aqueous solution, the characteristic excitation of Eu-PMA is 265 nm, and at the characteristic excitation wavelength, Eu-PMA has the characteristic emission of ligand PMA and metal ion Eu3+ at 435 and 617 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 A). Next, the effects of different excitation wavelengths on Eu-PMA were tested. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of Eu-PMA is different at different excitation wavelengths. When excited at the wavelength of 265 nm, Eu-PMA exhibits emissions at both 435 and 617 nm, with the highest fluorescence intensity peak near 617 nm and a relatively lower at 435 nm (Fig. 2B). To ensure high sensitivity and accuracy of the subsequent ratiometric fluorescence detection, the optimal excitation wavelength was selected as 265 nm, which is also the characteristic excitation wavelength of Eu-PMA solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism of Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent optical properties of lanthanide metal luminescent material Eu-PMA, a ratiometric fluorescence method was constructed to detect Pi. When the excitation wavelength is 265 nm, Eu-PMA system achieves dual fluorescence emission of ligand PMA and metal Eu3+ at 435 and 617 nm. Upon adding Pi, the fluorescence intensity of the system is significantly reduced at 617 nm, while that at 435 nm is slightly increased. Pi is successfully detected by ratiometric fluorescence analysis based on the ratio of intensity changes between 617 nm and 435 nm (Fig. 3 ).

Our previous investigations have shown that Pi has a relatively strong affinity for binding to the lanthanide ions Ln3+ [35]. Thus, we hypothesize that Pi binds competitively to the lanthanide ion Eu3+ to induce changes in the dual fluorescence signals of the Eu-PMA system. This is because the combination of Pi and Eu3+ destroys the structure of Eu-PMA and disrupts the interaction between PMA and Eu3+ (Fig. 3). Consequently, PMA cannot sensitize the luminescence of Eu3+, and the fluorescence is quenched. So, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ is significantly reduced at 617 nm, Meanwhile, for PMA at 435 nm, it is slightly increased, which is due to the presence of energy transfer [35]. The preceding analysis indicates that the competitive binding interactions between Pi and PMA with Eu3+ result in alterations of the double fluorescence signal, which provides conditions for Pi detection in Eu-PMA system by ratiometric fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for Pi detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To obtain more sensitive and accurate detection, the experimental conditions of Eu-PMA ratiometric fluorescence for detecting Pi were optimized. To attain Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence analysis, the Eu-PMA probe is required to have double emission fluorescence of the ligand PMA and Eu3+ at a suitable excitation wavelength. By testing the influence of characteristic excitation and emission spectra and different excitation wavelengths on the fluorescence properties of Eu-PMA, the optimal excitation wavelength at 265 nm was selected, and the Eu-PMA probe solution achieved double emission of ligand PMA and Eu3+ at 435 and 617 nm, respectively (Fig. 2). Next, the influence of pH for detecting Pi in Eu-PMA system was investigated, and the pH of the system was adjusted by Tris-HCl buffer solution. The difference of fluorescence intensity changes between the blank solution and the reaction system with Pi was compared under different pH conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 4
A, the largest difference in fluorescence intensity changes between 617 nm and 435 nm was observed at pH 7.5, providing a relatively reliable initial condition for ratiometric fluorescence detection. Subsequently, the variation in the fluorescence intensity ratio between the blank sample and the sample after adding Pi was tested. Although there is no significant difference in the fluorescence intensity ratio between blank solution and reaction system with Pi when pH = 7.5–9.5 (Fig. 4 B), the Tris-HCl buffer with pH = 7.5 is again selected considering that the actual sample test will be analyzed in a near-neutral environment. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of Pi detected by the Eu-PMA system at different pH were recorded. The results show that when pH = 7.5, there are two fluorescence emission peaks (ligand PMA at 435 nm, lanthanide ion Eu3+ at 617 nm), and the fluorescence is quenched at 617 nm and enhanced at 435 nm (Fig. 4 C). This is consistent with the feasibility analysis of the Eu-PMA ratiometric fluorescence detection of Pi. Therefore, the Tris-HCl buffer with pH = 7.5 was selected to regulate the acid-base environment of the system. Moreover, the reaction incubation time of the Eu-PMA system to detect Pi was tested and selected. When the incubation time is 20 min, the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (I 435/I 617) in the system tends to be stable, and when the reaction time is increased, the fluorescence intensity ratio does not change (Fig. 4 D), indicating that the system reaction is complete when the incubation time is 20 min. Therefore, the optimal reaction time is 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pi using Eu-PMA by ratiometric fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the above optimized detection conditions, Eu-PMA, a lanthanide luminescent material with excellent optical properties, was used as a fluorescent probe. Subsequently, the Eu-PMA sensor platform was constructed for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Pi. As observed in the fluorescence spectra, when Pi with concentration gradient is added to Eu-PMA system from small to large concentration, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-PMA system is significantly reduced at 617 nm (lanthanide ion Eu3+), but slightly enhanced at 435 nm (ligand PMA) (Fig. 5
A). Therefore, by simultaneously changing the fluorescence intensity of lanthanide ions and ligand (one decreasing, the other increasing), a ratiometric fluorescence method can be constructed to detect Pi. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 435/I 617) of the ligand PMA at 435 nm to that of Eu3+ at 617 nm serves as the ordinate, while the concentration of added Pi is represented on the abscissa. The ratio I 435/I 617 shows a good linear relationship with Pi concentration, and the linear equation is simulated. I 435/I 617 = 0.00189c + 0.10577, R = 0.996 and I 435/I 617 = 0.01181c − 0.18372, R = 0.998), the linear ranges are 0.1–90 μM (Fig. 5 B), to achieve the quantitative detection of Pi concentration. On the other hand, according to the 3σ rule (σ is the standard deviation of 12 empty solutions without Pi), we calculate that the minimum level of Pi detected by this ratiometric fluorescence method is 0.04 μM, demonstrating the high sensitivity of the Eu-PMA system for Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence method.

Considering the many and complex components in the actual sample, it is necessary to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the Eu-PMA system for Pi detection. To investigate the selectivity of Pi analysis in Eu-PMA system using ratiometric fluorescence method, some potential interfering substances were selected for corresponding control experiments. Under the identical experimental conditions, potential interfering substances with a concentration exceeding 10 times that of Pi were selected to be added to the Eu-PMA system, and their fluorescence spectra were tested after the reaction under excitation with a wavelength of 265 nm. When only Pi is added to the system, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 617 nm is quenched, whereas that of PMA at 435 nm is enhanced. In contrast, it is found that after the reaction of other potential interfering substances amplified by 10 times in the Eu-PMA system, there is no significant change in their fluorescence spectra compared with the blank solution without Pi added. In other words, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ is not quenched at 617 nm, nor is the intensity of PMA significantly enhanced at 435 nm (Fig. 5 C). In addition, I 435/I 617 has no significant change after the addition of other potential interferers to the Eu-PMA system compared with the blank solution, while that has a significant increase after the addition of Pi to the system (Fig. 5 D). This is due to the different changes in the two fluorescence signals of the system, that is, the fluorescence intensity is reduced at 617 nm, while is increased at 435 nm. Then, the anti-interference performance of Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence of the Eu-PMA system was investigated. When Pi is added to the solution simultaneously and the concentration of potential interfering substances is amplified by 10 times, it is found that the change of their fluorescence spectra was consistent with that of adding Pi alone to the Eu-PMA system. Compared with the blank solution, the fluorescence of Eu3+ is quenched at 617 nm, while PMA is increased at 435 nm (Fig. 5 E). The fluorescence intensity change ratio I 435/I 617 is close to the same (Fig. 5 F). The above studies indicate that Eu-PMA system has excellent selectivity and anti-interference for Pi detection by ratiometric fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pi in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility and practical application of Pi detection using the ratiometric fluorescence method, the Eu-PMA system was employed to measure Pi levels in water samples from the Jialing River, and a spiked recovery experiment was conducted. Jialing River water samples need to be filtered, centrifuged, and boiled. Firstly, the Pi content in water samples was directly detected as 3.28 μM in Eu-PMA system by this ratiometric fluorescence method. Next, Pi of different concentrations was added to Jialing River samples, and then recovery experiment was carried out by the ratiometric fluorescence method to test the change of fluorescence intensity and calculate the recovery rate. The results are shown in Table 1 . It is proved that the ratiometric fluorescence method is feasible and practical to detect the Pi content in river water samples by direct detection of Pi concentration and recovery experiment.

In this work, the lanthanide metal luminescent material Eu-PMA was synthesized by a simple solvothermal method and used as a probe to detect Pi by ratiometric fluorescence assay. The sensor system has the following advantages: the synthesis method of lanthanide luminescent material Eu-PMA is simple, and the Eu-PMA has excellent optical properties, with dual fluorescence emission (metal ion Eu3+ at 617 nm, ligand PMA at 435 nm) after excitation at 265 nm, which provides conditions for ratiometric fluorescence detection. Subsequently, Pi in the Eu-PMA sensing system is detected by ratiometric fluorescence method, indicating that Pi has a strong complexing ability with the ligand PMA, resulting in structural damage of Eu-PMA. Therefore, PMA cannot sensitize Eu3+ luminescence, and the fluorescence intensity at 617 nm is significantly reduced, while the ligand PMA is slightly increased at 435 nm. According to I 435/I 617 changes, a ratiometric fluorescence method was constructed to detect Pi, which improved the sensitivity and accuracy of Pi detection. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence method has high selectivity and anti-interference, and it has been successfully applied to the analysis and measurement of Pi content in Jialing River water samples, which proves the practical significance of the ratiometric fluorescence method to detect Pi, and broadens the application prospect of Pi in environmental water samples.

Xue Zhen Dong: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Tai Yi Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yu Rong Guo: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nian Bing Li: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hong Qun Luo: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (Nos. cstc2020jcyj-zdxmX0003, CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0924).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of serum electrolytes and phosphate after sodium phosphate colonoscopy bowel preparation: an evaluation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. J. Ainley, P. J. Winwood, J. P. Begley, Measurement of serum electrolytes and phosphate after sodium phosphate colonoscopy bowel preparation: An evaluation, Digest. Dis. Sci. 50 (2005) 1319-1323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Components of blood acid-base disturbance that accompanies urethane anaesthesia in rats during normothermia and hypothermia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Alfaro, L. Palacios, Components of blood acid-base disturbance that accompanies urethane anaesthesia in rats during normothermia and hypothermia, Clin. Exp. Pharmacol. Physiol. 24 (1997) 498-502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple fluorescent probe for fast and sensitive detection of inorganic phosphate based on uranine@ZIF-8 composite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, F. Fu, W. Yang, L. Ding, J. Dong, Y. Yang, F. Wang, Q. Pan, A simple fluorescent probe for fast and sensitive detection of inorganic phosphate based on uranine@ZIF-8 composite, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 301 (2019) 127110.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric detection of phytase enzyme activity and phosphate ion based on gelatin supported silver nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mashhadi Farahani, M. Dadmehr, M. Ali Karimi, B. Korouzhdehi, M. Amin Karimi, M. Rajabian, Fluorometric detection of phytase enzyme activity and phosphate ion based on gelatin supported silver nanoclusters, Food Chem. 396 (2022) 133711.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A case of severe osteomalacia secondary to phosphate diabetes in a renal transplant recipient

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sayegh, J.-F. Augusto, D. Chappard, P. Insalaco, J.-F. Subra, A case of severe osteomalacia secondary to phosphate diabetes in a renal transplant recipient, Int. Urol. Nephrol. 45 (2013) 1795-1799.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hyperparathyroidism and increased fractional excretion of phosphate predict allograft loss in long-term kidney transplant recipients

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Prakobsuk, S. Sirilak, K. Vipattawat, P. T. Taweesedt, V. Sumethkul, S. Kantachuvesiri, S. Disthabanchong, Hyperparathyroidism and increased fractional excretion of phosphate predict allograft loss in long-term kidney transplant recipients, Clin. Exp. Nephrol. 21 (2017) 926-931.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physiological regulation of phosphate by vitamin D, parathyroid hormone (PTH) and phosphate (Pi)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Jacquillet, R. J. Unwin, Physiological regulation of phosphate by vitamin D, parathyroid hormone (PTH) and phosphate (Pi), Pflugers Arch. Eur. J. Physiol. 471 (2019) 83-98.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low dietary inorganic phosphate affects the brain by controlling apoptosis, cell cycle and protein translation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Jin, S. K. Hwang, J. T. Kwon, Y. S. Lee, G. H. An, K. H. Lee, A. C. Prats, D. Morello, G. R. Beck, M. H. Cho, Low dietary inorganic phosphate affects the brain by controlling apoptosis, cell cycle and protein translation, J. Nutr. Biochem. 19 (2008) 16-25.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new fluorescent chemosensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of inorganic phosphate (Pi) in aqueous solution and living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Meng, Y. Wang, M. Yang, R. Zhang, R. Wang, Z. Zhang, A new fluorescent chemosensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of inorganic phosphate (Pi) in aqueous solution and living cells, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 53189-53197.
D. J. Conley, H. W. Paerl, R. W. Howarth, D. F. Boesch, S. P. Seitzinger, K. E. Havens, C. Lancelot, G. E. Likens, Controlling eutrophication: Nitrogen and phosphorus, Science 323 (2009) 1014-1015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Managing diffuse phosphorus at the source versus at the sink
